_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Randolph County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses that shape what genealogists can and cannot find.
display_name: Randolph County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: Original log courthouse replaced by a frame structure, then by a brick building. No fire or war damage to the early structures.
Mid-nineteenth-century brick courthouse survived the Civil War and served until replaced by the 1909 building.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Randolph County has suffered no major courthouse fire or war destruction. Records run continuously from the county's 1779 formation. The county was a center of Quaker and Unionist sentiment during the Civil War, and while guerrilla activity and Home Guard violence afflicted the county, the courthouse itself was not targeted. Records are generally well preserved.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Randolph County's record survival is excellent. If {ancestor_name} lived here after 1779, the civil records almost certainly survive. The county's Quaker heritage means that Friends meeting records may supplement the courthouse records with extraordinary detail.
record_losses.source: Blair, John L. Reminiscences of Randolph County. Asheboro: Courier Publishing, 1890.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Early volumes show age-related wear but remain legible.
deeds.repository: Randolph County Register of Deeds, Asheboro; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records collection

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records from formation
wills_estates.gaps: Some early loose estate papers are fragmentary. Bound will books are intact.
wills_estates.repository: Randolph County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NC State Archives digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds from county formation
marriages.gaps: Bonds more complete than registers for early decades. Register books begin approximately 1851.
marriages.repository: Randolph County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — County Court minutes from formation
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in earliest volumes. Superior Court minutes begin approximately 1807.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Randolph County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: NC State Archives microfilm; limited FamilySearch digitization

vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives for delayed birth certificates

substitute_sources.description: Randolph County, like neighboring Guilford, was a major center of Quaker settlement. Back Creek, Holly Spring, Marlborough, New Hope, and other monthly meetings kept birth, death, marriage, and removal records from the mid-eighteenth century. The Quaker Anti-Slavery movement and the resulting emigration of entire Quaker communities to Indiana, Ohio, and Iowa in the 1820s-1840s are documented in removal certificates that trace families across state lines.
substitute_sources.repository: Friends Historical Collection, Guilford College; some microfilmed at NC State Archives and FamilySearch
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Invaluable. Quaker meeting records provide birth dates, parentage, marriage details, and removal destinations with a precision that civil records of the era rarely match. The Quaker exodus to free states before the Civil War is documented in removal certificates that can trace a family from Randolph County to Indiana or Ohio.
substitute_sources.source: Hinshaw, William Wade. Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy, Volume I: North Carolina. Ann Arbor: Edwards Brothers, 1936.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Randolph County is enumerated from the first federal census. Agricultural and slave schedules (1850–1860) provide economic context, though Randolph had relatively few enslaved people compared to eastern NC counties.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families between land and court records. The small enslaved population in Randolph means the slave schedules are less voluminous but can be examined more thoroughly for individual identification.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Randolph County, NC.

substitute_sources.description: Randolph County was a center of anti-Confederate sentiment. Records of desertion, Unionist activity, Home Guard violence, and postwar loyalty oath filings document families whose wartime experience was the opposite of the Confederate narrative dominant in eastern NC. Court-martial records, amnesty petitions, and Southern Claims Commission files name Randolph County residents who actively opposed the Confederacy.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives (Southern Claims Commission files); NC State Archives; Randolph County Public Library local history collection
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Important for understanding family allegiances during the Civil War. A Randolph County ancestor who deserted the Confederate army or harbored Unionists may appear in records that tell a very different story than the standard Southern narrative.
substitute_sources.source: Auman, William T. Civil War in the North Carolina Quaker Belt: The Confederate Campaign Against Dissent in Randolph, Davidson, and Guilford Counties. Jefferson, NC: McFarland, 2014.

narrative_hooks: Randolph County's courthouse has never burned, and its records run unbroken from 1779 — if {ancestor_name} bought land, married, or appeared in court here, the paper trail almost certainly survives.

If {ancestor_name} was Quaker in Randolph County, the monthly meeting records may document the family in extraordinary detail — and if the family joined the great Quaker exodus to Indiana or Ohio in the 1820s-1840s, the removal certificate traces the journey from the Randolph County meetinghouse to the new one in the free state.

Randolph County was Unionist country during the Civil War — if {ancestor_name} deserted the Confederate army or sheltered those who did, the Southern Claims Commission files and court-martial records tell a story that the standard Southern narrative left out.

Before 1913, no North Carolina county required birth or death registration, so the absence of a birth record for {ancestor_name} in Randolph County reflects the era, not the family — Quaker meeting records, church rolls, and census schedules are the likeliest substitutes.
source_references: Blair, John L. Reminiscences of Randolph County. Asheboro: Courier Publishing, 1890.
Hinshaw, William Wade. Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy, Volume I: North Carolina. Ann Arbor: Edwards Brothers, 1936.
Auman, William T. Civil War in the North Carolina Quaker Belt: The Confederate Campaign Against Dissent in Randolph, Davidson, and Guilford Counties. Jefferson, NC: McFarland, 2014.
NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: North Carolina County Records. Raleigh, 1937–1942.
